evidence: Our original product was incredible for what it was, but it's kind of like a widget. It does its job really, really well, but it's not particularly innovative. And we sort of tapped out the amount of value that we can give our customers.

Not all of us want to look this fucking free. Some of us want to look like we have more things to do than a fully empty calendar. So I actually have to like my VC calendar... I have to have all these workarounds to make it look more busy than it is.
Over the years I've developed an intuition for how much I can help someone and more importantly how much help is even worth offering because sometimes it just ends up being more.
July launch: CRM + AI Executive System (scheduling + email automation). Philosophy: Relationship management, not productivity overall - not trying to compete with Superhuman.
Shane explains he's been at Calendly 3 years, recently became CPO.
Core ICP: People who sell their time (tax advisors, coaches, therapists). Key insight: These customers don't have CRMs - using hacked Notion or free HubSpot.
Shane will introduce V to Tobe (Calendly CEO). Potential senior role opportunity at Calendly.

source_display: Learning by Building as GTM Strategy
evidence: We build products for personal productivity that are built atop Zo. In building the productivity tool, you learn how to use Zo.
They come in and they only advise... 'oh you're missing this piece, you should add it. Goodbye.' Then the company can't do anything with that.
I'm afraid of like building something with Auth that has like a login and a state. Whoever solves elegant state for AI tools gets defensibility.
You need an economic incentive. What's better economic incentive than... this is going to help you make money? Directly help you make money is E-commerce. Indirectly help you make money is careers.
Zo is stuck between too technical for non-technical people and not technical enough for technical people. Proposed Solution: Economic incentive through careers (indirect) or e-commerce (direct).
Partnering with Jamie McDermott (Flow, Toronto) who has tools for call recording extraction. David's Arrangement: Partnering for execution capability.
V pitched GTM analysis to Tiffany at Zo: Direct economic incentive = E-commerce (Zo helps you make money). Indirect economic incentive = Careers (Zo helps you get a better job).
David initiated a meeting of the year conversation: Evolution from advisory role for Careerspan to becoming friends and exploring partnership opportunities.
